cards.title: Ditch the 'Smart' Traps: The Rise of DIY Hardware for Ultimate Control
cards.thesis: People are getting seriously good at building their own physical systems and integrating them with software, often to save a ton of money or gain full control over their devices. This hands-on hardware movement is blowing up because powerful, inexpensive components (like ESP32s, tiny computers you can program) are making it easy to create custom solutions that used to require expensive, locked-down commercial systems.

evidence.text: Someone replaced a $120,000 bowling alley system with just $1,600 worth of ESP32s (small, cheap computer chips), proving that complex systems can be rebuilt cheaply and effectively.
A builder shared that selling 2,500 MIDI recorders taught them 'Hardware is not so hard,' demystifying the process of creating physical products.

A user decided to kick their LG kitchen appliances off their network due to concerns about 'LG malware' and difficulty removing their account, highlighting a desire for more control and privacy over 'smart' devices.

There's interest in a modern USB-to-WiFi print server to convert old printers into wireless ones, allowing them to work with new devices without drivers, showing a need to bridge legacy hardware with modern tech.
People are nostalgic for the '2009 era of blogging about personal projects or super niche factoids,' indicating a yearning for hands-on, non-AI-focused creation.

cards.opportunity: Everyone's ditching expensive, closed 'smart' systems for cheap, open hardware like ESP32s, especially as they realize 'hardware isn't so hard.' The real opportunity isn't just making old printers WiFi-enabled, but creating super user-friendly, pre-configured kits for common, overlooked legacy systems—think small business point-of-sale peripherals, old school arcade cabinets, or even specialized farming equipment—that people can easily install to gain local control, cut costs, and avoid privacy headaches. Ship a simple hardware module and a mobile app that talks *only* to that module, and you've got a defensible wedge.

cards.title: Forget Google Analytics: The Micro-Niche for Privacy-First Revenue Tracking is Wide Open
cards.thesis: Builders are looking for alternatives to complex analytics tools like Google Analytics that also respect user privacy. While new 'privacy-friendly' options are emerging, they aren't yet capturing attention, suggesting a need for simpler, more focused solutions that truly solve a specific pain point for small businesses and creators.

evidence.text: Reignat offers privacy-friendly web analytics with realtime AI insights.
Kobbe provides privacy-friendly web analytics focused on traffic and revenue.

cards.opportunity: Everyone's trying to build privacy-friendly analytics, but the ones popping up aren't getting traction (0 engagement!). It feels like they're missing the point for indie builders who just want to know what's making them money *without* a headache or legal risk. An easy-to-set-up, privacy-first tracker that *only* focuses on connecting traffic sources to actual sales (e.g., via Stripe integration) would be huge, especially if it gave you super simple insights like 'this tweet brought in $X' instead of complex dashboards.
